## This website shows the data analysis for the COMP110 exercise 09. Analyzing the question of maybe switching quizzes online.

## Analyzation of data:

The visualizations show that higher quiz effectiveness ratings are associated with higher levels of student understanding. Basically, students who believe quizzes are more effective tend to report stronger comprehension of overall course material.

While this analysis doesn't prove a difference between online and in-person quizzes, the strong relationship between quizzes and understanding supports further exploration of online quizzes as a way to enhance learning to more difficult topics.

This analysis is based on self-reported survey data, which show how students see themselves in the course rather than how they're objectivally preforming. Also the survey does not indicate the format of quizzes.

Despite these limitations, the data suggests that quizzes play an important role in student understanding. Expanding quizzes to online formats may further support learning by better aligning assessments with real-world computer science.

## Conslusion

### Part 1.5: Conclusion

If your analysis of the data supports your idea, state your recommendation for the change and summarize the data analysixs results you found which support it. Additionally, describe any extensions or refinements to this idea which might be explored further. Finally, discuss the potential costs, trade-offs, or stakeholders who may be negatively impacted by this proposed change.

The analysis of the data supports my idea of moving to an online quiz format, as it shows the trend of students feeling quizzes are more effective when they feel they have a better understanding. The countplot visualizes this the most clearly, with an upward trend seen in the data. Of course with online quizzes there's a higher possibility of cheating but, with lockdown browsers and other accessable features, that can be solved. Also, VS Code naturally shows mistakes while coding, to combat this prompts can be more challenging and more open to student's creativty. This would better simulate how coding skills would translate in real-world experiences, and quiz students on important conceptual ideas while still allowing them to have fun with their coding.

You could better test this format with using both an online and a written portion and comparing how students preform and how much they feel they understand the concepts they're being tested on. However, this would cause a greater workload for TAs and instructors and make grading guidelines less clear which might negatively impact student as well. Overall, while the data supports the value of quizzes in promoting understanding, a move toward online quizzes should be done thoughtfully, with attention to accessibility, instructional workload, and student learning preferences.
